name: Totsuka-no-Tsurugi
shortDescription: A divine sword wielded by powerful deities in Japanese mythology, symbolizing strength and divine authority.
description: The Totsuka-no-Tsurugi (十拳剣), meaning "Ten-Hand Sword," is a prominent divine sword featured in Japanese mythology. It is not a single sword, but rather a type of sword wielded by several important deities. The Kojiki and Nihon Shoki, primary sources for Japanese mythology, mention its use by gods such as Izanagi and Susano-o. The sword is associated with great power and the ability to subdue chaos. Its name, referencing "ten hands," may indicate its substantial size or the immense strength required to wield it. The Totsuka-no-Tsurugi plays a crucial role in key myths, including Susano-o's slaying of the Yamata no Orochi and Izanagi's pursuit of Izanami in the underworld. The sword's association with these powerful deities and pivotal events solidifies its status as a symbol of divine authority and heroic prowess within the Shinto pantheon.
subtitle: Divine blade of gods and heroes.
meaning: Divine power, heroic strength, subduing chaos
origin: Japanese mythology, specifically the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki

extendedContent.title: Origins & Mythology
extendedContent.content: The Totsuka-no-Tsurugi's origins are deeply intertwined with the creation myths and early narratives of Japan. The sword appears wielded by Izanagi, the creator god, during his flight from Yomi, the underworld, after failing to retrieve his wife, Izanami. He used the Totsuka-no-Tsurugi to fend off the pursuing demons. Perhaps the most famous instance of its use is by Susano-o, the storm god, when he slays the Yamata no Orochi, an eight-headed serpent. After slaying the beast, Susano-o discovers the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi within the serpent's tail, further emphasizing the Totsuka-no-Tsurugi's role as a precursor to other significant divine weapons. The sword's appearances in these foundational myths establish its significance as a weapon of immense power and a tool for overcoming formidable adversaries.

extendedContent.title: Role & Significance
extendedContent.content: The Totsuka-no-Tsurugi holds a significant role in Shinto mythology and Japanese culture. It represents the power of the gods to establish order and defeat chaos. Its association with deities like Izanagi and Susano-o elevates it to a symbol of divine authority and heroic strength. The sword's presence in pivotal myths reinforces its importance as a cultural artifact representing the values of courage, determination, and the triumph of good over evil. In Shinto rituals and festivals, the Totsuka-no-Tsurugi, or representations of it, may be invoked to symbolize protection and the banishment of negative forces. Its significance extends beyond mere weaponry, embodying spiritual power and the capacity to overcome adversity.

extendedContent.title: Symbolism & Interpretation
extendedContent.content: The Totsuka-no-Tsurugi's symbolism is multifaceted. Its association with powerful deities signifies divine authority and the ability to enforce order. The sword's use in slaying monstrous creatures, such as the Yamata no Orochi, represents the triumph over chaos and the establishment of peace and harmony. The "ten-hand" aspect of its name can be interpreted as representing immense strength, dexterity or even the comprehensive nature of its power, capable of addressing problems from all angles. The sword can also be seen as a phallic symbol, representing fertility and creation, especially in the context of Izanagi's role as a creator god. Overall, the Totsuka-no-Tsurugi symbolizes power, authority, and the capacity to overcome challenges, both physical and spiritual.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: The Totsuka-no-Tsurugi continues to resonate in modern Japanese culture. It appears in various forms of media, including anime, manga, video games, and literature, often as a powerful weapon wielded by heroic characters. Its image evokes a sense of strength, tradition, and the enduring power of Japanese mythology. The sword's legacy extends to martial arts, where it serves as a symbol of skill, discipline, and the pursuit of perfection. The Totsuka-no-Tsurugi's enduring presence in popular culture and traditional arts demonstrates its continued relevance as a symbol of Japanese identity and cultural heritage. Its name and imagery are used to evoke a sense of history, power, and the enduring spirit of Japanese mythology.

sources.author: Anonymous
sources.description: The Kojiki is the oldest extant chronicle in Japan, containing myths, legends, and historical accounts.

sources.author: Anonymous
sources.description: The Nihon Shoki is the second-oldest book of classical Japanese history; more detailed and elaborate than the Kojiki.

associations.name: Metal
associations.significance: Swords are made of metal, symbolizing strength and durability.

associations.name: Order
associations.significance: The sword is used to defeat chaos and establish order.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Excalibur
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: A legendary sword associated with a powerful ruler, symbolizing authority and destiny.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Gungnir
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Odin's spear, a divine weapon representing power and authority.

companions.name: Susano-o
companions.description: The storm god who famously used the Totsuka-no-Tsurugi to slay the Yamata no Orochi.

companions.name: Izanagi
companions.description: A creator god who used the Totsuka-no-Tsurugi to defend himself in the underworld.

worshipPractices.name: Sword Dedication
worshipPractices.description: Swords are sometimes dedicated to Shinto shrines as offerings or symbols of devotion.

festivals.name: None Specific
festivals.description: While no specific festivals are dedicated solely to the Totsuka-no-Tsurugi, swords in general are revered during certain Shinto festivals.

cultural.modernLegacy: The Totsuka-no-Tsurugi is a popular motif in Japanese pop culture, appearing in anime, manga, and video games as a symbol of power and heroism.
